---
title: "Portland Metropolitan Area, Oregon"
year: 2023
image: /images/portland_OR.jpg
image-alt: "Image of Portland, Oregon"
categories: ["Transfer/MRF/Transport", "Service Procurement", "Education/Training"]
---
{{< include _return-to-pf.qmd >}}

::: {.article}

# 2023 Service Agency Transfer Station Cost of Service Study

# 2019 Transfer Station Contractor Operator Procurement

# 2016 Transfer Station Cost of Service Study

# 2012 Transfer Station Repair & Replacement Study

Bell & Associates was a subcontractor to URS for the transfer station repair
and replacement study. Bell & Associates completed the financial analysis of
the study of Metro’s two transfer stations and closed landfill. The study
reviewed the facilities and equipment at all three sites to identify what
repairs and replacements are necessary during the next 20 years. Bell &
Associates reviewed the current fund balances for transfer stations as well as
the closure account for the St. John’s Landfill to determine if the current
balances and contributions were adequate to fund future operational needs and
asset replacement.

# 2008 Transfer Station Repair & Replacement Study

# Solid Waste Rate Seminar

Bell & Associates was contracted to conduct an in-house solid waste rate
seminar for Metro staff. The purpose of this project was to improve Metro's
institutional knowledge of the region's local solid waste rate-setting
practices so that Metro staff can conduct more robust analyses of ratepayer
impacts. 

# System Oversight

Bell & Associates reviewed the financial and operational data of secondary
solid waste handlers (transfer stations and landfills) licensed by Metro prior
to 2003. The objective of the engagements was to provide oversight and ensure
the accurate reporting of information and collection of fees to Metro. 

# Residential Food Collection Program Analysis

Bell & Associates in conjunction with Applied Compost Inc. worked with local
governments and composting facilities to determine the impact of a residential
food collection program. If a residential organics system were implemented, the
yard debris and organics collected together would most likely be sent to the
one fully-permitted and operational organic waste composting facility located
within the Portland metropolitan region. The study reported on the economic and
structural impact to existing residential collections systems, composting
facilities, compost markets, transportation systems, and the ancillary effects
(job losses or job creation, lost business, lower tax base, etc.).

[Thumbnail Image by Cacophony - Own work, CC BY-SA 3.0](https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=2335737)

:::
